title: Why Pakistan Struggles With Violence Against Women
gist: Mariyam Suleman Anees notes Pakistan ranked last (148th of 148) in the WEF's Global Gender Gap Report 2025, with its parity score slipping slightly, and argues that despite decades of legislation and activism, violence against women remains widespread and public spaces remain unsafe for women and girls.

insights.claim: The World Economic Forum's Global Gender Gap Report 2025 ranked Pakistan last — 148th of 148 countries — with its overall gender-parity score slipping from 57 percent in 2024 to 56.7 percent in 2025.
insights.speaker: Mariyam Suleman Anees
insights.so_what: A hard, worsening benchmark for ESG and social-risk screening on Pakistan exposure, useful for tracking whether policy attention translates into measurable gains.
insights.locator: The Global Gender Gap Report 2025 by the World Economic Forum

insights.claim: Despite decades of legislation and activism by organizations such as U.N. Women in Pakistan and Oxfam, violence against women remains widespread in Pakistan and public spaces remain difficult for women and girls to navigate safely.
insights.speaker: Mariyam Suleman Anees
insights.so_what: Signals limited measured impact from existing legal and advocacy interventions to date, relevant for donors and NGOs calibrating expected returns on Pakistan-focused programming.
insights.locator: Statistics and lived experiences point toward inequalities

insights.claim: Gender disparity and violence against women remain among the most politically contentious subjects in Pakistani public discourse.
insights.speaker: Mariyam Suleman Anees
insights.so_what: Flags social and political friction that can complicate policy reform or corporate/NGO initiatives tied to gender issues operating in Pakistan.
insights.locator: Yet, gender disparity, discussions of women's rights
